title: Raw Data vs Engineered Features
concept: A single raw value like a timestamp can be broken apart into multiple new features — like day_of_week, is_weekend, and hour_of_day — each surfacing a pattern the raw value hid.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does "garbage in, garbage out" tell us about model quality?

options.text: It's capped by the quality of the features, no matter how advanced the algorithm is
It depends only on how much computing power is available
It's unrelated to the features used

exercises.explain: Right! So if feature quality matters this much, how do you actually go about creating a good feature? Let's see it in action.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A more advanced algorithm can fully compensate for meaningless, low-signal features.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Today, we'll turn one ordinary raw value into several genuinely useful ones.

cards.title: A number that looks like nothing
cards.body: 2024-03-15T14:32:00. To a model, that raw timestamp is just one long number. But hiding inside it are several genuinely useful clues — if you know how to pull them out.

cards.title: What is feature creation?
cards.body: Feature creation is deriving new, more useful variables from raw data by extracting or combining the signal already hidden inside it — turning one raw value into several meaningful ones.

cards.title: A detective examining one clue
cards.body: A detective doesn't treat a single clue as useless just because it looks ordinary — they examine it from different angles to extract several leads. Feature creation does the same to a raw value: examine it from different angles to pull out several separate clues.

cards.title: One timestamp becomes three features
cards.body: From 2024-03-15T14:32:00, you can derive: day_of_week = Friday (useful for spotting weekly patterns), is_weekend = no (useful for spotting weekday-vs-weekend behavior), and hour_of_day = 14 (useful for spotting daytime-vs-night behavior). One raw value, three new signals.

cards.title: The raw value isn't automatically useless
cards.body: It's easy to assume a raw timestamp is just as informative as the features derived from it. In reality, a model often struggles to find weekly or daily patterns in one raw number — the created features can surface patterns the raw value was hiding.

cards.title: Raw Timestamp vs. Derived Features
cards.body: One raw value, several new signals:

options.text: Deriving new, more useful variables from raw data by extracting the signal hidden inside it
Deleting raw data columns that look complicated
Choosing which algorithm a model will use
Collecting more rows of the same raw data

exercises.explain: Exactly! Feature creation pulls new, useful signals out of a raw value.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A single raw timestamp can be used to derive several separate features, like day_of_week and hour_of_day.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! One raw value can hide multiple distinct, useful signals.

exercises.sentence: From a raw timestamp, you might create the feature is_weekend to flag whether a date falls on a ___.
exercises.explain: Right! is_weekend is a new feature derived from the raw timestamp, flagging Saturday/Sunday specifically.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best represents a feature CREATED from a raw timestamp, rather than the raw timestamp itself?

options.text: hour_of_day: 14, derived from 2024-03-15T14:32:00
2024-03-15T14:32:00, stored exactly as logged
A random customer ID number unrelated to any timestamp

exercises.explain: Correct! hour_of_day is a new, derived signal pulled out of the raw timestamp — the other two aren't created features at all.

exercises.prompt: A ride-share app wants to predict demand and only has a raw pickup timestamp for each trip. Why might deriving day_of_week and hour_of_day help, rather than using the raw timestamp alone?

options.text: They surface repeating patterns, like Friday evening rush hours, that the single dense raw value hides
They make the raw timestamp column disappear from the dataset entirely
They guarantee the model will never overfit
They have no effect on what patterns the model can find

exercises.explain: Right! Repeating weekly/daily patterns are much easier for a model to catch once they're pulled out as their own features.

exercises.prompt: A retailer has one raw column: the exact date and time of every purchase. They want a model to predict weekend sales spikes but performance is flat. What's the most likely explanation, based on today's lesson?

options.text: The raw timestamp alone hides the weekend pattern — deriving a feature like is_weekend would likely surface it
Weekend sales spikes are impossible to predict with any amount of feature creation
The raw timestamp already IS the same thing as an is_weekend feature
The problem must be the algorithm, not the features, since timestamps are always sufficient on their own

exercises.explain: Exactly! A raw timestamp buries the weekend pattern inside a single dense value — deriving is_weekend pulls that exact signal out where the model can use it.

exercises.prompt: Given the raw value 2024-07-04T09:15:00, which set of derived features correctly follows the day_of_week / is_weekend / hour_of_day pattern from today's lesson?

options.text: day_of_week: Thursday, is_weekend: no, hour_of_day: 9
day_of_week: 2024, is_weekend: yes, hour_of_day: July
day_of_week: 09:15:00, is_weekend: Thursday, hour_of_day: no
A single unchanged copy of 2024-07-04T09:15:00

exercises.explain: Right! July 4th, 2024 falls on a Thursday, so is_weekend is no, and the time 09:15 gives an hour_of_day of 9. Next: what helps you decide WHICH features are worth creating in the first place?
